summary: The branch has zero commits ahead of origin/main, so assessment used current main/trunk state. Existing container-job code provides job/ownership/correlation/expiry labels, ownership-collision checks, lifecycle cleanup on terminal paths, a separate durable cleanup outcome, evidence-before-cleanup ordering, credential-directory cleanup, and no image/global-prune cleanup command. However, cleanup is limited to the container and credential directory; removal is not idempotent for absent containers and does not re-check ownership immediately before deletion; there is no durable-state-backed recurring orphan reconciler, fail-closed ownership-source gate, dry-run/pass limits/leases/backlog metrics, comprehensive object registry, or the requested recovery/integration/security coverage. New implementation work is required.

requirements.description: Every job-created runtime object carries immutable MoonMind job ownership labels/metadata — partially present on containers (job, ownership, correlation, expiry), but object kind, backend ref, creation time, schema/version, and equivalent metadata for all other job objects are absent.

Normal success, failure, timeout, and cancellation attempt idempotent cleanup of all job-scoped objects — the workflow always attempts container removal and credential cleanup, but does not cover networks, scratch/staging objects or registered objects, and container removal is not already-absent idempotent.
Cleanup outcome is durable and separate from primary workload/publication outcome — ContainerJobStatus/WorkflowResult have a separate cleanup AuxiliaryOutcome projected after cleanup.

Repeated cleanup treats already-absent resources as success and never targets a replacement object with mismatched ownership — create/reconcile reject ownership collisions, but remove_container directly issues docker rm --force without a final ownership check and treats absence as failure.

The recurring reconciler removes only labeled objects whose durable job ownership and terminal/expiry state are confirmed — no container-job recurring durable-state reconciler exists; the legacy DockerContainerJanitor uses daemon labels and TTL only.
The reconciler refuses destructive action when the job store/Temporal ownership source is unavailable, ambiguous, or inconsistent — no such reconciler or durable ownership-source check exists.
Dry-run, per-pass object/byte/time limits, leases, and bounded diagnostics are supported — not implemented for container-job cleanup/reconciliation.

Active/nonterminal jobs and their objects are protected — normal workflow cleanup follows terminal determination, but the legacy TTL janitor does not consult durable job state, so reconciler-level protection is missing.

Shared images, approved shared caches, durable artifacts, workspaces, unrelated Docker resources, and MoonMind service containers are never removed by job cleanup — evidence is published before cleanup and current job cleanup targets a deterministic container/auth directory without image deletion, but full resource-class/deployment ownership safeguards are not implemented.

No job cleanup path issues global prune or image-removal commands — inspected container-job cleanup uses docker rm for the job container and filesystem credential cleanup; no image/global-prune command is present.

Temporary registry credentials and staging files are included in cleanup/reconciliation without leaking their values — registry auth is redacted and removed immediately plus terminal cleanup, but temporary input/output staging files and reconciler recovery are absent.
